title: The Paddock of Fence-Post Annotations

features.name: the biro arrows themselves
features.desc-on-touch: fence post to fence post, each bearing one word in fading biro, pointing the direction of a sentence nobody has walked in full. reading them, you're not sure if they spell instruction or prayer

features.name: the wooden posts
features.desc-on-touch: weathered hardwood, decades old, slowly being reclaimed by the earth. the annotations are becoming corrupted—words are fading, new marks appearing, as if the posts are rewriting themselves

features.name: the comparative readings
features.desc-on-touch: the archive has begun to file these posts together with similar fragments from other regions—a sentence in a river voice, the same words in a paddock, a variation appearing in a service station ledger

features.name: the gap posts
features.desc-on-touch: two posts missing from the line—where they stood, only holes remain. the archive knows what words belonged there but can no longer read them directly

features.name: the final post
features.desc-on-touch: the line ends at a rusted gate. the last post holds the final word, but the arrow points beyond the fence into land the archive cannot reach—at least not yet

inscription: ERROR: metaphor detected in storage layer—the fence posts are both markers and a message, both guide and the thing being guided

file.name: Fence_Line_Survey_1978_Maintenance_Notes_Annotated.txt
file.note: original boundary survey documents, but each word of the survey now cross-references a biro-marked post, and the posts reference back, creating a loop the archive is still mapping

exits.label: back toward the river recording, following the arrow-line backwards
the gate at the line's end, pointing beyond into unreachable land
a service road parallel to the posts, marked on old maps
the riverbank where the posts' words sometimes echo in water-sound
